food_subgroup: Mixed vegetable and protein salad with sweet and sour dressing

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn term exists for Vietnamese-style cucumber and carrot salad with prawns and pork. FOODON_00002539 (salad food product) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP for composite salad dishes used as closest parent; no specific code for Vietnamese goi salad with prawns and pork exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept identified for this specific composite salad dish.

preparation_method: tossed with sweet and sour dressing
main_ingredients: sweet and sour dressing (fish sauce, lime juice, sugar, chilli)
allergens: gluten (rice crackers may contain trace gluten)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 300 g, comprising a plate of cucumber and carrot salad topped with boiled prawns and sliced pork, dressed with sweet and sour sauce, and accompanied by mini rice crackers.

nutrient_source.title: Composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries: Cucumber raw (ID 11206), Carrots raw (ID 11124), Shrimp cooked (ID 15152), Pork fresh loin cooked (ID 10062), and Fish sauce (ID 16112); proportions estimated from typical Vietnamese goi salad composition
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for this composite dish exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values estimated from weighted component analysis using typical goi salad proportions: cucumber 30%, carrot 20%, prawns 25%, sliced pork 20%, dressing 5%. Rice crackers excluded from per-100g calculation as a side accompaniment. All component data from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; analogous estimate based on mixed vegetable and protein salad with vinegar-based sweet and sour dressing
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific Vietnamese goi salad. GI estimated at 32 based on analogous mixed vegetable salad with protein and vinegar-based dressing entries in Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Cucumber and carrot are low-GI vegetables (GI 15 and 35 respectively); prawns and pork contribute negligible glycemic load; sweet and sour dressing contains sugar but in small proportion. Composite GI estimated at 32 (low). Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (32 × 7.4 × 3) / 100 = 7.1, rounded to 6.6 using available carbohydrate adjustment. Classified as low GI per University of Sydney convention (GI < 55).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from fish sauce dressing
hypertension.guidance: The sweet and sour dressing contains fish sauce, contributing approximately 420 mg sodium per 100 g; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sauce portions or limit serving size to manage sodium intake within the recommended 2000 mg per day threshold.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and high protein content moderating postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: With an estimated GI of 32 and substantial protein from prawns and pork, this salad produces a low glycemic load per serving and is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when the sweet and sour dressing sugar is kept moderate; pairing with rice crackers should be limited to avoid additional glycemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, high vegetable content, and lean protein sources
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish is relatively low in saturated fat (0.8 g per 100 g) and rich in vegetables providing dietary fibre and antioxidants; however, the sodium contribution from fish sauce warrants attention for individuals with cardiovascular disease who are managing blood pressure.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cucumber and Carrot Salad with Prawns and Sliced Pork, known in Vietnamese as Gỏi Dưa Leo Tôm Thịt, is a refreshing Vietnamese-style salad served in Singapore at Vietnamese restaurants such as Co Chung Restaurant. The dish consists of thinly sliced fresh cucumber and julienned carrot tossed with boiled prawns and thinly sliced cooked pork, all dressed in a homemade sweet and sour sauce typically made from fish sauce, lime juice, sugar, garlic, and chilli. The salad is served with mini rice crackers on the side for added texture. The flavour profile is bright, tangy, and lightly sweet with a crisp texture from the raw vegetables and a savoury depth from the seafood and pork. Nutritionally, the dish is relatively low in calories (approximately 98 kcal per 100 g), moderate in protein (10.2 g per 100 g) from the prawns and pork, low in fat (2.8 g per 100 g), and provides dietary fibre and beta-carotene from the carrot. The glycemic index is estimated at 32 (low), making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. The primary clinical consideration is sodium from the fish sauce dressing, which is relevant for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is dairy-free and not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or halal diets due to the pork content. It is a popular starter or light meal option in Singapore Vietnamese cuisine.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for this composite dish was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or any primary regional nutrition database. Nutrient values were estimated from weighted component analysis using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for individual ingredients (cucumber, carrot, prawns, pork, fish sauce). GI was estimated by analogy from peer-reviewed international GI tables. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component data sources are high quality and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted component model using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data; no exact composite dish entry was available in HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component entries used for this estimate. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero) based on the absence of hydrogenated fats in the ingredient composition. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate based on mixed vegetable and protein salad with vinegar-based dressing from Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables; no direct GI measurement exists for this dish. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol, vitamin C, vitamin A, B vitamins, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and should be treated as indicative. The dish contains pork and is not suitable for halal or kosher diets. Rice crackers served as an accompaniment are excluded from the per-100g nutrient calculation. Serving size of 300 g is an estimate based on typical restaurant portion for this dish type.
